entries.title: Joint Training Is A Controlled Evaluation, Not A Public Tournament

coordinates.label: Post-All Might society before the Paranormal Liberation War

continuity.sourceBoundary: My Hero Academia manga chapters 1-430. Chapter 431 'More' is treated as an optional post-series extra overlay unless explicitly loaded.
continuity.notes: Anime-only scenes, movies, OVAs, Vigilantes, Team-Up Missions, light novels, games, and filler material are excluded from the primary deck family unless added as separate overlay decks.

content.fact: Joint Training is a school-controlled combat exercise between Classes 1-A and 1-B, with teachers judging teamwork, adaptation, rescue logic, and combat decisions.
content.injection: Use Joint Training as a closed U.A. exercise. Keep the stakes educational and tactical, not public-celebrity pressure like the Sports Festival.

sourceInfo.title: My Hero Academia
sourceInfo.notes: Draft Saga Lorecard; requires human canon review before reference release.

entries.title: Class 1-B Has Serious Tactical Parity With Class 1-A

coordinates.label: Post-All Might society before the Paranormal Liberation War

continuity.sourceBoundary: My Hero Academia manga chapters 1-430. Chapter 431 'More' is treated as an optional post-series extra overlay unless explicitly loaded.
continuity.notes: Anime-only scenes, movies, OVAs, Vigilantes, Team-Up Missions, light novels, games, and filler material are excluded from the primary deck family unless added as separate overlay decks.

content.fact: Class 1-B is not a joke class; its students bring organized counterplay, trained combinations, and strong Quirk matchups against Class 1-A.
content.injection: Do not write Class 1-B as automatic fodder. Their edge comes from teamwork, planning, and unfamiliar Quirk interactions.

content.fact: Hitoshi Shinso participates in Joint Training as a serious transfer candidate, using training under Aizawa to prove he can function in Hero Course combat.
content.injection: When Shinso appears here, treat him as an evaluated outsider trying to earn Hero Course standing, not as a settled Class 1-A member.

ability.name: Brainwashing

antiLore: Do not make Shinso control people silently without activation setup.

entries.title: Aizawa And Vlad King Frame The Exercise Differently

content.fact: Aizawa watches Class 1-A with critical restraint while Vlad King openly advocates for Class 1-B, shaping the exercise as both training and rivalry.
content.injection: Use Aizawa and Vlad as evaluators with different temperaments: Aizawa is dry and severe, Vlad is louder and visibly biased toward 1-B.

content.fact: Jurota Shishida’s Beast Quirk pressures Class 1-A with strength, senses, and pursuit, requiring restraint and deception instead of simple direct hits.
content.injection: Against Shishida, emphasize pressure from strength and tracking senses. Counterplay should use misdirection, immobilization, or teamwork.

entries.title: Joint Training Ends With Class Growth Rather Than Final Ranking Certainty

coordinates.label: Post-All Might society before the Paranormal Liberation War

continuity.sourceBoundary: My Hero Academia manga chapters 1-430. Chapter 431 'More' is treated as an optional post-series extra overlay unless explicitly loaded.
continuity.notes: Anime-only scenes, movies, OVAs, Vigilantes, Team-Up Missions, light novels, games, and filler material are excluded from the primary deck family unless added as separate overlay decks.

content.fact: The Joint Training outcome demonstrates growth, weaknesses, and tactical variety across both classes rather than permanently proving one class superior.
content.injection: After Joint Training, keep the lesson developmental: students learn matchups, weaknesses, and growth paths instead of settling Class 1-A vs 1-B forever.
